title: Your First Logical Proof
description: Work through a classic logic puzzle and build your first formal argument using natural deduction.

steps.title: A classic puzzle
steps.instruction: Read the puzzle: On an island, everyone is either a knight (always tells truth) or a knave (always lies). You meet two people. A says: 'At least one of us is a knave.' B says nothing. What are A and B?
steps.prompt: Before reasoning formally: what is your gut answer? Write it down.

steps.title: Set up the cases
steps.instruction: There are 4 possible combinations: (A=knight, B=knight), (A=knight, B=knave), (A=knave, B=knight), (A=knave, B=knave). Evaluate each.
steps.prompt: What does A's statement mean in each case? Is the statement true or false for each combination? Write out all four.

steps.title: Apply the knight/knave rules
steps.instruction: A knight's statement must be TRUE. A knave's statement must be FALSE. Eliminate combinations that contradict these rules.
steps.prompt: Which combinations are consistent? Which are eliminated? What must be true of A and B?

steps.title: Write the argument in standard form
steps.instruction: Write your conclusion as a numbered argument: Premise 1, Premise 2, ... Therefore: Conclusion.
steps.prompt: Draft the argument in standard form. Include the rule you used at each step.

steps.title: Identify the argument type
steps.instruction: Was this deductive or inductive? Valid or sound? What inference rule did you apply at the key step?
steps.prompt: Name the reasoning type and explain why this argument is valid (or check if it is).

steps.title: Reflect and extend
steps.instruction: You just did logical reasoning. The same method applies to evaluating claims, debugging code, and reading arguments.
steps.prompt: Where in your life could you use this kind of systematic case analysis? Give one example.
